messages.meaning: fpmsyncd successfully established a TCP socket connection to zebra's FPM server; route programming pipeline between FRR and APPL_DB is now active
likely_causes: Normal startup sequence after bgp container starts
zebra restarted and fpmsyncd reconnected
Warm restart reconciliation beginning
next_steps: Verify FPM connection is stable: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status fpmsyncd
Confirm zebra is running: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status zebra
Check route table is populating: show ip route summary
Verify APPL_DB is receiving routes: redis-cli -n 0 KEYS 'ROUTE_TABLE:*' | wc -l

messages.meaning: fpmsyncd lost its TCP connection to zebra's FPM server; new routes from FRR will not reach APPL_DB or the ASIC until reconnection
likely_causes: zebra process crashed or was restarted
bgp container OOM kill or supervisord restart
FPM port 2620 blocked by iptables or namespace issue
fpmsyncd itself hit an exception and tore down the socket
next_steps: Check zebra status: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status zebra
Check fpmsyncd status: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status fpmsyncd
Look for zebra crash in logs: docker exec bgp cat /var/log/frr/frr.log | grep -i 'crash\|assert\|abort' | tail -20
Check FPM port is listening: docker exec bgp ss -tnlp | grep 2620
Verify routes are stale by comparing kernel to APPL_DB: ip route show | wc -l vs redis-cli -n 0 KEYS 'ROUTE_TABLE:*' | wc -l
Restart fpmsyncd if zebra is healthy: docker exec bgp supervisorctl restart fpmsyncd

messages.meaning: fpmsyncd is writing a route entry to ROUTE_TABLE in APPL_DB; this is the normal path for FRR-learned routes entering the SONiC pipeline
likely_causes: BGP session received a new prefix
Static route configured and activated
Warm restart reconciliation replaying routes
next_steps: Confirm route reached APPL_DB: redis-cli -n 0 HGETALL 'ROUTE_TABLE:<prefix>'
Check orchagent picked up the route: redis-cli -n 1 HGETALL 'ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_ROUTE_ENTRY:{"dest":"<prefix>"}'
Verify end-to-end: show ip route <prefix>

messages.meaning: fpmsyncd is removing a route entry from ROUTE_TABLE in APPL_DB; orchagent will subsequently withdraw the route from the ASIC
likely_causes: BGP withdrew the prefix (peer down, policy change, MED change)
Static route removed from config
Interface carrying the next-hop went down
next_steps: Confirm route was removed from APPL_DB: redis-cli -n 0 EXISTS 'ROUTE_TABLE:<prefix>'
Check if the BGP session is still up: show bgp summary
Check if next-hop interface is up: show interfaces status
Verify ASIC removal: show ip route <prefix> (should be absent or via different path)

messages.meaning: fpmsyncd could not write a route to APPL_DB due to a Redis error; routes are silently dropped and will not reach orchagent or the ASIC
likely_causes: redis-server process crashed or restarted mid-write
Redis memory limit hit (maxmemory policy evicting keys)
Unix socket /var/run/redis/redis.sock not accessible from bgp container
Redis AUTH mismatch after container restart
next_steps: Check redis-server health: docker exec database redis-cli ping
Check Redis memory: docker exec database redis-cli info memory | grep used_memory_human
Check Redis error log: docker exec database cat /var/log/redis/redis-server.log | tail -30
Verify socket is accessible from bgp container: docker exec bgp ls -la /var/run/redis/redis.sock
Check if routes are missing from APPL_DB: redis-cli -n 0 KEYS 'ROUTE_TABLE:*' | wc -l
Restart fpmsyncd to force route replay from zebra: docker exec bgp supervisorctl restart fpmsyncd

messages.pattern: Failed to parse.*FPM|fpm.*malformed|invalid FPM message|FPM msg.*length
messages.meaning: fpmsyncd received a FPM netlink message from zebra that it could not parse; the route update is discarded and will not reach APPL_DB
likely_causes: FRR version mismatch between zebra and fpmsyncd -- netlink message format incompatibility
Memory corruption in zebra causing garbled FPM output
Partial write to FPM socket (kernel buffer issue)
Custom FRR patch introducing new message fields fpmsyncd does not understand
next_steps: Check FRR version consistency: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show version'
Check fpmsyncd binary version: docker exec bgp fpmsyncd --version 2>&1
Look for zebra assertion failures near the timestamp: docker exec bgp cat /var/log/frr/frr.log | grep -i 'assert\|FPM' | tail -30
Enable FPM debug in zebra: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'debug zebra fpm'
Check if specific prefixes trigger parse errors by correlating BGP updates: show bgp ipv4 unicast
Restart both zebra and fpmsyncd to reset the FPM socket: docker exec bgp supervisorctl restart zebra fpmsyncd

messages.meaning: fpmsyncd is performing post-restart route reconciliation -- replaying all routes from zebra to APPL_DB to ensure consistency after a warm restart
likely_causes: Warm restart enabled and bgp container restarted
fpmsyncd restarted while zebra remained up (partial restart scenario)
SONiC warm reboot sequence initiated
next_steps: Monitor reconciliation progress: show warm_restart state
Watch APPL_DB route count stabilize: watch -n 2 "redis-cli -n 0 KEYS 'ROUTE_TABLE:*' | wc -l"
Do not make route policy changes until reconciliation completes
If reconciliation hangs: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status fpmsyncd
Check reconciliation timer has not expired: journalctl -u bgp -n 50 | grep reconcil

messages.meaning: fpmsyncd received a route from zebra with a next-hop that cannot be resolved to a valid interface or neighbor; the route is dropped and will not be programmed into the ASIC
likely_causes: Recursive next-hop chain too deep or contains a loop
Next-hop IP is not reachable via any directly connected interface
Interface carrying the next-hop went down before zebra withdrew the route
ECMP next-hop group partially invalid (some members down)
VRF misconfiguration causing next-hop lookup in wrong table
next_steps: Identify the problematic prefix from logs and inspect next-hop: show ip route <prefix>
Check if next-hop is reachable: ping <nexthop-ip> from management or control plane
Verify neighbor resolution: show arp | grep <nexthop-ip>
Check zebra's view of the next-hop: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip nexthop'
Check if the upstream BGP peer is advertising reachable next-hops: show bgp neighbors <peer> advertised-routes
For VRF routes: verify next-hop VRF is correct with show ip route vrf <vrf-name>
